title: Brandy snaps
subtitle: Lace biscuits rolled while hot, filled with whipped cream
excerpt: Golden, lacework biscuits made from butter, sugar, golden syrup, and flour. They bake flat, are rolled around a wooden spoon handle while still hot, and are filled with whipped cream once cool. The window for rolling is about 30 seconds.
sourceNotes: Brandy snaps appear in British baking guides from the 19th century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, documents the snap biscuit. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records the rolled and cream-filled form. The name refers to the ginger snap family rather than brandy as an ingredient; traditional recipes often contain no brandy. The rolled-on-a-spoon technique for shaping them is documented from the Victorian period.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The unfilled brandy snap cylinders keep in an airtight tin for up to 3 days. Fill within 2 hours of serving; cream softens the snap after that.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Bake only 4 at a time; shaping must happen while hot.

glossaryTerms.definition: As soon as brandy snaps come out of the oven, they are flexible for about 30 seconds before they set hard. Working quickly, lift one disc at a time from the tray using a palette knife or thin spatula, and roll it around the handle of a wooden spoon. Hold for 5 seconds, then slide off and rest seam-side down on the rack. If a snap sets before rolling, return it to the oven for 30 seconds to soften again.

content.text: The entire skill of brandy snaps is timing. Once the tray comes out of the oven, every snap has a 30-second window before it sets.
requires working biscuit by biscuit, not pan by pan. Bake no more than four at a time until you are confident.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Melt the butter, golden syrup, and sugar together in a saucepan over low heat until the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat. Sift in the flour and ginger and stir until smooth. Add the lemon juice. The mixture will be thin and loose.

Drop 4 teaspoons of mixture onto the tray, well apart (at least 8 cm between each). Bake for 7 to 8 minutes until each disc is golden brown throughout and the surface has a bubbled, lacy appearance. Remove from the oven. Working quickly, lift one disc at a time and roll it tightly around the handle of a wooden spoon. Hold for 5 seconds, then slide off and rest seam-side down on the rack. If a disc sets before you reach it, return it to the oven for 30 seconds. Repeat with the remaining mixture.
Whip the cream with the icing sugar and vanilla to firm peaks. Transfer to a piping bag. Pipe cream into both ends of each snap cylinder just before serving.
